sourceMovie.title: Dirty Dancing
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Dirty Dancing (1987) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Emile Ardolino's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The drama and music blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Step Up
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Dirty Dancing clicked for you, Step Up (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and music, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Dirty Dancing?
faq.answer: Try Step Up, Footloose, The Last Song—each shares DNA with Dirty Dancing while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Dirty Dancing?
faq.answer: Dirty Dancing (1987) is best described as Drama, Music, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Dirty Dancing part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Dirty Dancing Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
